coredata.dc:title: A novel sensor array with ability to respectively identify phenol and ketone for the precise discrimination of origins of raw Pu-erh and its counterfeiting

Zhen, Linxue
Li, Jie
Jin, Peize
Gu, Yu
Ma, Yu
Song, Donghui
Yang, Xiaoyu
Li, Yongxin
Huang, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Most reported sensor arrays for teas were based on the sensing of phenolic hydroxyl group on tea polyphenols. In this work, a novel sensor array was developed based on the simultaneous sensing of phenols and ketones, for the enhanced discrimination of tea polyphenols with/without ketone, and then for the efficient discrimination of raw Pu-erh teas from different origins and the counterfeit, combined with machine learning. This sensor array is consisting of four channels. Channel A is carbon dots, room-temperature carbon nanoparticles (RT-CNPs), whose fluorescence can be quenched by ketone; Channel B is the chromogenic agent, 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH), which can combine with ketone to undergo absorption spectral changes. Tea polyphenols without ketone have little effect on Channel A and Channel B. Channel C (RT-CNPs + nanozyme) and Channel D (DNPH + nanozyme) are the addition of nanozyme with polyphenol oxidase activity to Channel A and Channel B. The nanozyme can catalyze the oxidation of phenols to the quinones, which means Channel C and D can react to tea polyphenols with phenols or ketones. Based on the different quenching efficiency of various tea polyphenols on the carbon dots, differences in the color due to the ketone combination with DNPH, and differences in the degrees of tea polyphenol’s oxidation by the nanozyme, the proposed four-channel sensor array achieved the enhanced discrimination of tea polyphenols. This sensing technology which can simultaneously recognize phenols and ketones, has an excellent applicating prospects in the recognition and distinguish of tea and its derivatives (such as tea drinks), with oxidation and transformation of tea polyphenols.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sensor array
Raw Pu-erh
Tea polyphenols
Ketone
Nanozyme

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nanozyme and carbon nanoparticles
Construction of dual-model sensor arrays for the identification of TPs with phenol and ketone in raw Pu-erh
The tea samples used as detecting objects and the preparation of adulteration samples
Discrimination of raw Pu-erh’s origins and its adulteration
Characterizations and properties of RT-CNPs and GMP-Cu
The discrimination of TPs based on sensor array
Differentiation of raw Pu-erh tea origin and its counterfeiting based on the sensor array

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A novel sensor array with ability to respectively identify phenol and ketone for the enhanced discrimination of origins of raw Pu-erh.
(A) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of RT-CNPs; (B) the validation of the polyphenol oxidase-like activity of GMP-Cu.
Four channels’ responses to TPs: (A) Channel A; (B) Channel B; (C) Channel C; (D) Channel D.
The response of sensor array to 50 μM TPs: (A) fingerprint, (B) heatmap. The distinguish of 50 μM TPs based on (C) LDA and (D) HCA.
The LDA distinguish of TPs in different concentrations: (A) 100 μM, (B) 40 μM, (C) 30 μM, (D) 20 μM, (E) 10 μM and (F) 5 μM.
(A-C) The LDA discrimination of TP at different concentrations; (D-F) the liner correction between Factor 1 and concentrations.

The concentration-independent distinguish: accuracy of (A) train set and (B) test set based on 6 machine learning algorithms; (C) The stability of RF algorithms; (D) Importance of individual sensing units in recognition based on RF algorithm.
The confusion matrix of (A) train and and (B) test data in the concentration-independent distinguish.

(A) The response of sensor array to raw Pu-erh tea from different origins and fake raw Pu-erh tea: fingerprint; (B) heatmap; the distinguish of raw Pu-erh tea and raw Pu-erh tea from different origins based on (C) LDA and (D) HCA.
Distinguish for different levels of adulteration of raw Pu-erh based on (A) LDA, (B) HCA and (D, E) RF; (C) Correlation between adulteration rate and Factor1 in LDA.

head.ce:title: A novel sensor array with ability to respectively identify phenol and ketone for the precise discrimination of origins of raw Pu-erh and its counterfeiting

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Groundwater Resources and Environment (Jilin University)
College of New Energy and Environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Most reported sensor arrays for teas were based on the sensing of phenolic hydroxyl group on tea polyphenols. In this work, a novel sensor array was developed based on the simultaneous sensing of phenols and ketones, for the enhanced discrimination of tea polyphenols with/without ketone, and then for the efficient discrimination of raw Pu-erh teas from different origins and the counterfeit, combined with machine learning. This sensor array is consisting of four channels. Channel A is carbon dots, room-temperature carbon nanoparticles (RT-CNPs), whose fluorescence can be quenched by ketone; Channel B is the chromogenic agent, 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine (DNPH), which can combine with ketone to undergo absorption spectral changes. Tea polyphenols without ketone have little effect on Channel A and Channel B. Channel C (RT-CNPs + nanozyme) and Channel D (DNPH + nanozyme) are the addition of nanozyme with polyphenol oxidase activity to Channel A and Channel B. The nanozyme can catalyze the oxidation of phenols to the quinones, which means Channel C and D can react to tea polyphenols with phenols or ketones. Based on the different quenching efficiency of various tea polyphenols on the carbon dots, differences in the color due to the ketone combination with DNPH, and differences in the degrees of tea polyphenol’s oxidation by the nanozyme, the proposed four-channel sensor array achieved the enhanced discrimination of tea polyphenols. This sensing technology which can simultaneously recognize phenols and ketones, has an excellent applicating prospects in the recognition and distinguish of tea and its derivatives (such as tea drinks), with oxidation and transformation of tea polyphenols.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: With the potential biological and medicinal benefits (Popowski et al., 2024; Xu, Zhang, Zhang, Wei, & Zhou, 2024), tea is the second most consumed beverage with large amount of tea polyphenols (TPs). During the tea processing and storage, TPs would be oxidized to the substance with ketone (Chen et al., 2024; Tanaka et al., 2009). Compared to other tea, raw Pu-erh tea is richer in contents, such as the substance with phenols and substance with ketones (Wang et al., 2018; L. Zhang, Li, Ma, & Tu, 2011). The contents of raw Pu-erh tea vary widely depending on its origin.

There are many cases of counterfeiting and adulteration in the tea industry. According to Li et al.(Li, Elliott, Petchkongkaew, & Wu, 2024), in the EU Rapid Alert System for Food and Feed (RASFF) and the FoodAkai database for the last decade, tea counterfeiting and adulteration accounted for a relatively large number of tea fraud cases. The most common tactic is the complete or partial substitution of one type of tea for another, which also happened in the field of raw Pu-erh. Only large-leaf sun-dried green tea produced in Yunnan can be called raw Pu-erh(Xiong et al., 2023), but due to the similarity of the ingredients and the same processing techniques, there are some illegal vendors who sell large-leaf sun-dried green tea produced outside Yunnan Province, posing it as raw Pu-erh (Lv et al., 2014).

The traditional methods to distinguish raw Pu-erh tea are based on the large instruments, such as gas chromatography-mass spectrometry (Lv et al., 2014), high performance liquid chromatography (Zhang et al., 2011). The other ways to achieve this goal are based on the sensory evaluation (Ren et al., 2022), but the evaluator tends to be affected by environmental factors (Tong et al., 2022; Xiong et al., 2023).

Sensor arrays are known for their ability to distinguish similar analytes and things in complex environments (Song, Tian, et al., 2024;Xu et al., 2024). There are a few sensor arrays applied to the differentiation of tea (Jia, Pan, Zhou, & Zhang, 2021; Ni et al., 2023; Yang, Bi, et al., 2024; Yang, Zou, et al., 2024). Jia (Jia et al., 2021) et al developed a colorimetric sensor array to discriminate Chinese teas based on the differences between TPs. With the machine learning and indicator displacement assay, Ni (Ni et al., 2023) et al achieved the goal of distinguishing 16 kinds of tea. But the principle of the above sensor arrays is based on the discrimination of TPs. It still has some limitation. If there is an oxidation transformation from phenol to quinone, such as fermentation and storage, the accuracy will be declining. Li et al (Li et al., 2022) reported the sensor array for detecting the fermentation degrees of black tea with an 87 % correctness rate. Zhang et al (Zhang, Yuan, Ren, Ning, & Wang, 2023) discriminated the freshness of green tea with an 85 % correctness rate.

In the process of constructing sensor array, there will be channels added to obtain more information. With the increase of sensing units, the dimensions and complexity of data will increase. To deal with this issue, machine learning algorithms are introduced. Based on its dimensionality reduction capability and great data processing capability, machine learning can better distinguish data, and make predictions about it (Li, Zhang, et al., 2024; Song, Zou, et al., 2024).

With the great stability, low cost and modifiability (Dou et al., 2024;Wu et al., 2024), nanozymes are potential elements for the construction of sensor arrays(Li, Zhao, Wang, & Li, 2023; Luo et al., 2024). The sensor arrays based on nanozymes are applied into many fields, such as pesticide detection(Han et al., 2024;Zhu et al., 2020), food(Huang et al., 2023;Wu et al., 2023), and medicine(Han, Zhang, Chen, & Li, 2018; Zhang et al., 2024). Due to the mass phenol groups in tea(Lei, Yang, Song, Huang, & Li, 2022), copper-based nanozymes with polyphenol oxidation activity (PPO) have been applied in the construction of sensor array for the distinguish of tea (Yang, Bi, et al., 2024; Yang, Zou, et al., 2024).

In this work, we constructed a four-channel sensor array ( Fig. 1
,
Table S1
). Channel A is the RT-CNPs which can be quenched by TPs with ketone due to the electron-absorbing ability(Liu et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2018). Channel B is the chromogenic, DNPH, which can combine with ketone producing color changes(Cao & Tarr, 2017; Fan et al., 2020;Li, Fang, LaGasse, Askim, & Suslick, 2017). TPs without ketone have little effect on Channel A and Channel B. In addition, Channel C (RT-CNPs + nanozyme) and Channel D (DNPH + nanozyme) are the addition of nanozyme guanosine 5′−monophosphate-copper (GMP-Cu) with PPO to Channel A and Channel B. The nanozyme can catalyze the oxidation of phenols to the quinones, which means Channel C and Channel D can response to TPs with phenols or ketones. The quenching effects of different TPs on the RT-CNP vary due to the structure of the ketones, so the colors produced between ketone and DNPH vary. Also, the nanozyme would also oxidize TPs to different degrees. Based on the above differences, the proposed four-channel sensor array achieved the enhanced discrimination of TPs and raw Pu-erh. Combined with the machine learning, the sensor array with the capability of recognizing TPs with phenols or ketones can make a concentration-independent distinction of TPs with/without ketones with 100 % accuracy. The method has an excellent applicating prospects in the recognition and distinguish of tea and its derivatives with oxidation and transformation of TPs.

The following were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China): 2-Morpholinoethanesulphonic acid (MES), CuCl2·2H2O, NaCl, MgCl2, (+)-Catechin (C), (−)-epigallocatechin (EGC), Gallic acid(GA), (−)-Epigallocatechin gallate(EGCG), Caffeic acid (CA), Quercetin (Que), Kaempferol (Kae), Apigenin (Api), Luteolin (Lut), O-phenylenediamine (OPD), Guanosine 5′-monophosphate (GMP), Ammonium persulfate, Tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), sucrose, glutamic acid (Glu), 4-Aminoantipyrine (4-AP), and 2,4-Dinitrophenylhydrazine(DNPH). All of these chemical reagents were used without further processing.

Ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu Suzhou Instruments Mfg. Co., Ltd. China). Chroma Meter (CR-400, Konica Minolta. INC. Japan). High speed centrifuges (HC-2062, Anhui USTC Zonkia Scientific Instruments Co., Ltd. China). CNC ultrasonic cleaner (KQ-100DE, Kunshan Ultrasonic Instruments Co., Ltd. China). RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanozyme and carbon nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the nanozyme, GMP-Cu, was conducted referring to previous work(Huang, Li, Hao, Yu, & Li, 2021; Y. Yang et al., 2022). To be brief, the reagents were added as follows: 100 μL 100 mM Tris-HCl buffer at pH 8.5, 560 μL H2O, 200 μL 10 mmol/L GMP, 140 μL 50 mM CuCl2. After the appearance of sediment, it was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min, then washed for three times and finally decentralized in water.

The RT-CNPs, was synthesized according to Chen’s work with slight modifications(Chen, Liu, Yu, & Wang, 2019). Briefly, 0.1 M ammonium persulphate was dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water. Then, 5 mL 0.1 M OPD aqueous solution was slowly dropped into the above solution at room temperature, which would generate precipitates. After incubation at room temperature for 24 h, the formed sediment was collected and washed three times with deionized water. Then, it would be dissolved in deionized water and stored at 4 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of dual-model sensor arrays for the identification of TPs with phenol and ketone in raw Pu-erh
ce:section.ce:para: There are four sensing units consisting of the sensor arrays. All the TPs were dissolved into the 80 % ethanol–water solution, which was as same as the solution used in the extraction of tea. For the Channel A, 100 μL TPs only with phenol (EC, C, EGC, EGCG, GA, and CA) and with ketone (Kae, Que, Api, and Lut) were added to the solution containing 90 μL 0.5 mg/mL RT-CNPs, 600 μL 100 mM Tris-HCl buffer at pH 7.3 and 210 μL H2O for 10-min incubation. For the Channel B, 1 mL of detecting system contained 150 μL NaOH (1 mM), 40 μL DNPH (1 mM), 510 μL H2O and 100 μL TPs. The ketone would combine with DNPH. It would be incubated for 3-min before the test. For the Channel C, 100 μL TPs were added to the solution containing 500 μL 100 mM MES at pH 6, 60 μL 1 mg/mL GMP-Cu, 120 μL 0.5 mg/L RT-CNPs and 200 μL H2O for 10 min. For the Channel D, 1 mL of detecting system contained 400 μL Tris-HCl (100 mM, pH 7.5), 100 μL GMP-Cu (1 mg/mL), 60 μL DNPH (1 mM), 340 μL H2O and 100 μL TPs. It would be incubated for 20 min at 50 ℃ before the test. After the incubation, Lab values of colorimetric channels and fluorescence intensity of fluorescence channels were recorded. All the data was collected twelve times (3 parallels*4 duplicates). |ΔA|/A0 was used as the signal to construct the machine learning model. |ΔA| indicates the absolute value that the blank control value minus the value of the test substance. A0 stands for the values of blank, the values stand for the fluorescence intensity or the L, a, b values obtained from four channels. The data analysis was further processed in IBM SPSS Statistics 21 software and Python 3.11.5. To test the anti-interference performance of the arrays, the interference substance (five times the concentration of TPs) was added to the sensor array at the time of incubation. Among the used algorithms, linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA) were proceeded by the IBM SPSS Statistics 21 software’s analyze function, K Nearest Neighbors (KNN), random forest (RF), artificial neural network (ANN), support vector machine (SVM), and decision tree (DT) were conducted based on Python 3.11.5′s sklearn frame. The training set and the test set were randomly assigned in a 9:1 ratio. Among the above machine learning algorithms, HCA was conducted according to the rule that clustering is prioritized between samples with the closest Euclidean distance. Based on HCA, KNN introduces the concept of labeling (e.g., names of samples). DT is based on nodes containing different judgment criteria to differentiate goals. And RF contains multiple DT algorithms. The idea of LDA's differentiation is to vary the matrix of the target so that it minimizes the intra-label distance and maximizes the differentiation between labels. The idea of SVM is similar to LDA, but is based on the comparison with the hyperplane. ANN is based on the mimicry of neuronal networks in the human brain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The tea samples used as detecting objects and the preparation of adulteration samples
ce:section.ce:para: The green tea from 4 origins and raw Pu-erh from 6 origins were chosen as the test objectives. The specific origins and their abbreviations are shown in Table S2 . Tea samples from each origin are purchased from two stores to eliminate errors. The total number of samples is 20 (10 origins*2 stores). All the tea samples are in the first class and were made in 2023. All the green teas used as the counterfeit raw Pu-erh were sun-dried and made from large-leaf tea leaves (Camellia sinensis var), which is similar to the green tea in the raw Pu-erh tea process. To test the discriminating ability of the adulteration samples, two tea samples were chosen as the adulteration (green tea from Guizhou) and the real one (raw Pu-erh from Bingdao). The adulteration samples were prepared as follow: two tea samples (raw Pu-erh from Bingdao, green tea from Guizhou) were blended according to a certain mass ratio mass ratio (Bingdao:Guizhou = 10:0, 8:2, 6:4, 4:6, 2:8, 0:10), and two replicates of each proportion of the blended samples were carried out to eliminate errors. The total number of adulteration samples is 12 (6 ratios*2 replicates).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Discrimination of raw Pu-erh’s origins and its adulteration
ce:section.ce:para: In order to extract as many TPs as possible, the 2 g tea was extracted by 40 mL 80 ethanol aqueous solution in 60 °C for 10 min, according to a previous paper(Friedman, Levin, Choi, Kozukue, & Kozukue, 2006). Then, the infusion was filtered by filter paper and 0.22 μM PTFE filter and diluted 10 times before analysis. TPs in section 2.3 were replaced by the above infusion to collect colorimetric and fluorescence signal to distinguish the origins of these tea. The way to collect signals were the same to that in 2.3.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterizations and properties of RT-CNPs and GMP-Cu
ce:section.ce:para: GMP-Cu and RT-CNPs were prepared referring to the previous paper(Chen et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2021;Yang et al., 2022). As shown in Fig. S1 , the Atomic Force Microscope (AFM), Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM), and Fourier Transform Infrared Spectrometer (FTIR) images indicated that the synthesized nanozyme and carbon nanoparticles are consistent with the reported materials in terms of morphology and chemical composition, which indicates the successful synthesize of GMP-Cu and RT-CNPs.

To invest the fluorescence capability of RT-CNPs, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra was collected ( Fig. 2
A). RT-CNPs has its maximum excitation wavelength at 425 nm. Under that excitation, the corresponding maximum emission wavelength is at 578 nm, which is same to the results of origin research(S. Chen et al., 2019). The PPO of GMP-Cu was investigated by the colorimetric reaction between 2,4-DP and 4-AP (Fig. 2 B). The orange line indicates GMP-Cu has the great PPO activity(Huang et al., 2021).

The responses of TPs to four channels were collected. The selected TPs were shown in Fig.S2 . They can be classified as TPs with ketone, which are Kae, Que, Api, Lut, and TPs without ketone, such as EC, C, EGC, EGCG, GA, and CA. These 50 μM TPs were added to the four channels. As shown in the fluorescence spectra of Channel A ( Fig. 3
A), TPs with ketone can greatly quench the fluorescence of RT-CNPs (Liu et al., 2019;Wang et al., 2018;Zhu et al., 2021), but this Channel has little response to TPs without ketone, due to the weak quenching ability of phenol in weakly alkaline environment. Because the structure around the ketone would affect the electron-absorbing ability of ketone, the quenching rates of TPs with ketone differ(Liu et al., 2019). In the ultraviolet–visible absorbance spectra of Channel B ( Fig. 3 B), TPs with ketone can bind to DNPH and it can create strong absorbance peaks(Cao & Tarr, 2017; Fan et al., 2020; Li et al., 2017). Meanwhile, there is little response from TPs without ketone. The position and strength of absorbance peaks change depending on the structure of TPs with ketone(Li et al., 2017). In the fluorescence spectra of Channel C ( Fig. 3 C), the RT-CNPs’ fluorescence intensity at 578 nm decreases differently with the addition of TPs. The nanozyme (GMP-Cu) oxidizes the phenolic hydroxyl group on TPs to quinone in different degrees. Due to the different oxidation levels, the produced oxidation products and the ketone can quench the fluorescence differently. It can produce differences among fluorescence intensities of RT-CNPs at 578 nm. The ultraviolet–visible absorbance spectra of Channel D ( Fig. 3 D) shows that combination between the quinone oxidized from phenol/ketone and DNPH produce different absorbance curve. The above results show that Channel A and B can only response to ketone, Channel C and D can response to both ketone and phenol. Meanwhile, the responses in channels are different.

According to the above responses, a fingerprint could be created ( Fig. 4
A). |ΔA|/A0 is used as the signal of fingerprint. In this signal, |ΔA| indicates the absolute value that the blank control value minus the value of the test substance, A0 stands for the values of blank, the values stand for the fluorescence intensity or the L, a, b values obtained from four channels. Channel A and Channel C use the fluorescence intensity as features. Intensity1 stands for the fluorescence intensity of Channel A, Intensity2 stands for the fluorescence intensity of Channel C. Channel B and Channel D use the L, a, b values as features. L1, a1, b1 stand for the L, a, b values of Channel B, L2, a2, b2 stand for the L, a, b values of Channel B. So, the total number of features in all channels is 8.

To make the differences more straightforward, the fingerprint was transformed to heat map ( Fig. 4 B). It can be seen that |ΔA|/A0 values of TPs with ketone are much higher than that of TPs without phenol in Channel A and Channel B, which suggests the possibility to discriminate TPs with/without ketone. After that, machine learning algorithms, linear discriminant analysis (LDA) and hierarchical cluster analysis (HCA), were introduced to distinguish TPs.

As shown in Fig. 4 C, TPs could be well distinguished in 10 circles by LDA with 100 % cross-validation accuracy. Meanwhile, TPs with ketone could be separated from TPs without ketone. It indicates the sensor array can differentiate TPs with and without ketone. To further test the ability of above two capabilities, the unsupervised algorithm HCA was introduced ( Fig. 4 D). It is discovered that every kind of TPs can be correctly clustered hierarchically from bottom to top. At the same time, TPs with ketone can be clustered firstly and separated from TPs without ketone. Based on the above results, 50 μM of TPs are successfully distinguished by LDA and HCA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The discrimination of TPs based on sensor array
ce:section.ce:para:
To further test the quantitative ability of sensor array, seven concentrations of the same TPs were distinguished ( Fig. 6
, Fig. S4-6 ). It can be seen that different concentrations of the TPs can be distinguished with 100 % cross-validation accuracy. Since Factor 1 in the typical scoring plot is higher than 90 %, the relationship between Factor 1′s values and the concentrations was investigated ( Fig. 6 , Fig. S4-6 ). The value of factor 1 showed a strong linear relationship with the concentration, and the R2 values of the fitted equations were all bigger than 0.990. It suggests that the response of every channel has a correlation with the concentrations of TPs.

The above are the respective distinctions among ten TPs at the same concentrations or seven concentrations of the same TPs. When multiple concentrations and kinds of TPs were present at the same time, the concentration-independent distinguish of TPs should be processed. Concentration-independent distinguish means that all the concentrations of a test substance are put together as a label to differentiate. For example, all concentrations of EGCG (5–100 μm) are put together as a label (EGCG) to distinguish from other polyphenols. Firstly, it was processed by LDA, which performs not well with a low 80 % correct rate ( Fig. 7
A). To solve this matter, we further put serval machine learning models into use. Compared to traditional algorithms (LDA, HCA), the selected machine learnings methods are less susceptible to overfitting and has a better generalization performance.

The used algorithms were as follow: K Nearest Neighbors (KNN), random forest (RF), artificial neural network (ANN), support vector machine (SVM), and decision tree (DT). As shown in Fig. 7 A and Fig. 7 B, the correctness rates obtained by the five algorithms are higher than that of LDA. Among the five algorithms, the correctness rates processed by KNN, RF and ANN are bigger than 99 % and close to 100 %.

Among these three algorithms, KNN, ANN, and RF, RF has the smallest variance, indicating that it is the most stable among the above algorithms. In order to further test the stability of RF, we conducted multiple repetitions, and found that the correct rate of train set and test set varied little in 20 repetitions ( Fig. 7 C). Meanwhile, the above results show that RF has high accuracy and stability, so the concentration-independent distinguish was conducted by RF. The ability to conduct the concentration-independent distinguish with high accuracy should be contributed to the apply of these algorithms, which are seldom used in other paper discriminating the phenols(Jing et al., 2024;Xu et al., 2024).

According to the confusion matrix, it can be found that the concentration-independent distinguish based on RF has a 100 % correct rate ( Fig. 8
A,B). Finally, the contribution of each unit to the concentration-independent distinguish by RF was investigated ( Fig. 7 D). Except for L1 of Channel B, L2 of Channel D, all the other signals have large contribution to the concentration-independent distinguish (bigger than 10 %), which indicates the reasonable construction of the sensor array.

Next, the sensor array's anti-interference ability was tested. Several substances commonly found in tea were randomly chosen as potential interferents (mineral salts: NaCl, MgCl2; free amino acids: glutamic acid; soluble sugars: sucrose) and added to the system at five times the concentration of TPs, and were found to have little effect on the discrimination results of the sensor array ( Fig. S8 A-D). The selectivity was tested by adding these interfering substances to the system in place of TPs and recording the response from 4 channels. It revealed that the sensor array response little to them, indicating good selectivity ( Fig. S8 E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differentiation of raw Pu-erh tea origin and its counterfeiting based on the sensor array
ce:section.ce:para: Raw Pu-erh tea is richer in contents, such as the substance with phenolic hydroxyl groups (the catechins, phenolic acids) (Zhang et al., 2011) and substance with ketones (the flavonoids, the oxidation products from phenols) (Wang et al., 2018). The contents of raw Pu-erh tea vary widely depending on its origin. According to Wang’s research(Wang et al., 2018), the contents of TPs with/without ketone in raw Pu-erh from different origins differed significantly and could be used as a basis for distinguishing the origins. Among above TPs, contents of EC, EGC, and glycosidic compounds of kae, que vary most depends on the origins. JM, KL had the least amount of TPs without ketone (EC, EGCG), but more TPs with ketone (Kae, Que). Comparatively, BD and XG had more TPs without ketone (EC, EGCG), and fewer TPs with ketone (Kae, Que). TPs content of BZ and NN were between them. Only large-leaf sun-dried green tea produced in Yunnan can be called raw Pu-erh, but some unscrupulous traders will sell large-leaf sun-dried green tea from other places as raw Pu-erh for counterfeiting(Lv et al., 2014). Due to the similar ingredient and the same process, it would be hard for the normal consumers to tell the differences between them(Lv et al., 2014), though it might be possible for the trained ones to visually distinguish the above teas(Tong et al., 2022). Based on the distinguish of TPs with/without ketone, the four-channel sensor array had been applied to distinguish the origins and counterfeiting of raw Pu-erh.

In this experiment, four large-leaf sun-dried green teas from other origins were chosen as counterfeit raw Pu-erh (hereafter referred to as “fake raw Pu-erh ”). Four fake raw Pu-erh and raw Pu-erh from six origins were chosen as detection objects. The tea infusion was added to the system. The responses from four channels were recorded. From the obtained fingerprint and heatmap ( Fig. 9
A and 9B), the differences between raw Pu-erh tea and fake raw Pu-erh tea can be clearly shown.

Two algorithms (LDA, HCA) were used to achieve the differentiation of the origins of raw Pu-erh tea and fake raw Pu-erh tea. With LDA discrimination, all the teas were correctly discriminated with 100 % cross-validation accuracy ( Fig. 9 C). Meanwhile, raw Pu-erh teas and fake raw Pu-erh teas can be separated from each other with HCA discrimination ( Fig. 9 D), the teas from the same origins can be clustered together firstly. Raw Pu-erh teas can be clustered, separated from fake raw Pu-erh tea.

On the basis of the above discriminations, adulteration experiments were conducted. Fake raw Pu-erh tea from Guizhou was chosen as adulteration, to adulterate BD raw Pu-erh. The discrimination rate was 100 % based on both LDA and HCA algorithms ( Fig. 10
A, B). Since Factor 1 in the typical scoring plot is higher than 90 %, the relationship between Factor 1 values and the adulteration rates was investigated ( Fig. 10 C). The values of Factor 1 have a strong liner correlation with the adulteration rates (R2 = 0.994). To further test the ability of RF model, the adulteration distinguish was also processed by RF ( Fig. 10 D, E). It turns out that RF model has an excellent performance in the distinguish of adulteration, with a 100 % correctness. The anti-interference experiments for actual detection were carried out (
Fig. S12
). It was found that sensor array still has good anti-interference ability in raw Pu-erh tea distinguish.

The four-channel sensor array was developed with the ability of simultaneous recognition of phenols and ketones. Two algorithms (LDA and HCA) were applied to achieve the differentiation of 10 TPs at different concentrations and the differentiation of groups they have. Compared to our previous report (Yang, Bi, et al., 2024; Yang, Zou, et al., 2024), this sensor array can discriminate more TPs due to the Channels recognizing the ketones. The concentration-independent discrimination of TPs progressed from 75 % correct at the beginning to 100 % correct. The progress should be attributed to the use of these algorithms, which are less susceptible to overfitting, has a better generalization performance and seldom used in other paper discriminating the phenols (Jing et al., 2024; Xu et al., 2024). Based on the above research, the distinguish of raw Pu-erh origins was further studied, where there is little sensor array applied. Four fake raw Pu-erh teas and six Pu-erh teas were introduced as samples. They can be distinguished with a 100 % correct rate. Therefore, the sensor array for simultaneous recognition of phenols and ketones is proposed for the first time, it is applied to the origin differentiation of raw Pu-erh tea and its counterfeiting. Unfortunately, this sensor array has not been converted into a smart detector device for field detection. And the construction of sensor array is still a bit cumbersome, with the possibility of optimization.

Zhichun Bi: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Project administration, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Linxue Zhen: Writing – review & editing, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jie Li: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Methodology. Peize Jin: Writing – review & editing. Yu Gu: Validation, Investigation. Yu Ma: Validation, Investigation. Donghui Song: Visualization. Xiaoyu Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Yongxin Li: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Hui Huang: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (22176069), and Natural Science Foundation of Jilin Province (20240402022GH, 20220101042JC and 20220101263JC).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodres.2024.115371.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Indicator displacement assay for freshness monitoring of green tea during storage

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Y., Yuan, W., Ren, Z., Ning, J., & Wang, Y. (2023). Indicator displacement assay for freshness monitoring of green tea during storage. Food Research International, 167, 112668. doi: 10.1016/j.foodres.2023.112668

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioinspired Nanozymes as Nanodecoys for Urinary Tract Infection Treatment

(2024). Bioinspired Nanozymes as Nanodecoys for Urinary Tract Infection Treatment. ACS Nano, 18 (12), 9019-9030. doi: 10.1021/acsnano.3c12783

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A fluorescent sensor array-based electronic tongue for Chinese tea discrimination

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Zhu et al., 2020
